name: The Stall That Keeps No Queue
alias: the 3am supper stall · the kopi you must never finish to the bottom of the cup
body: You smell it before you see it, charcoal and pork lard and something burnt-sweet, threading down a row of hawker units that should all be shuttered at this hour, their roller grilles down and padlocked, the floor still wet from the cleaner's hose. One stall is lit. Its tube light is warmer than the others, more yellow, the colour kopitiams glowed in before everything went LED, before the cooked-food centres were upgraded and tiled and given their bright new signboards, and the steam off the wok rises in a single unhurried column with no wind to trouble it, no ceiling fan turning, no late bus groaning past on the main road to stir the air. There is no queue. There is never a queue, and in a country where the good stalls always have one, where people will stand forty minutes in the heat for char kway teow and call it worth it, the emptiness in front of this stall is the loudest thing about it. The plastic stools sit stacked and untaken. The drinks-stall fridge two units down hums to no one.

The uncle, or the auntie, never quite faces you while taking the order, working the wok with a back turned and a wrist that knows the motion, and the food comes faster than food can be made, set down steaming before you have finished fishing the coins from your pocket. The rule is in the eating, not the buying: you may sit, you may taste, you may even praise it the loud appreciative way the regulars do, but you must not finish what is in front of you. Leave the last spoonful of broth, the final corner of kaya toast, a swallow of kopi at the bottom of the cup. It is the bottom of the cup that announces itself; the closer you sip toward it, the more the night around you seems to lean in and the further away the main road sounds, the traffic thinning to nothing, the cicadas cutting out, until there is only the scrape of your own spoon. To scrape the plate clean, to drink to the dregs, to tilt the cup the last degree the way you would at any honest supper, is to settle the bill in the currency the stall actually keeps, and the price is not the coins you leave under the saucer.

What lingers is the kindness of the place, how full it leaves you, how warm, how reluctant your legs are to carry you home through the quiet estate. People who have eaten there say the satisfaction stayed for days and so did the craving, a pull back toward a stall they could never find again in daylight, walking the same row of units at noon to find only a shuttered space and a unit number nobody seems to rent. The detail that fixes it as real is the receipt, or what passes for one: a torn scrap of pink ration-style paper, the kind no stall has issued in fifty years, the kind your grandfather might have been given queuing for rice, with the price written in a careful old hand in cents, and on the back, faint in pencil, the name of a coffeeshop that was demolished before you were born, its corner long since become a carpark, then a void deck, then a stranger's window.
note: This one drifts between the supper crowds of Geylang, the back lanes off Jalan Besar, and the 24-hour prata and bak chor mee places where the taxi drivers and the night-shift nurses overlap around three. The delivery riders tell a leaner version: a stall that pings up on no app, takes no orders, and is gone from the spot by sunrise. It surfaces on the food forums every few months, always sworn to by a friend of a friend.

Variants disagree on what must be left, some say the broth, some the very last grain of rice, the Hokkien tellers say you must leave a mouthful for the ones who cannot eat. The ward everyone agrees on is simple and it is the oldest table manner there is: leave a little behind, never finish, and do not go back looking. Almost certainly a story stitched from old offerings to hungry ghosts and the plain strangeness of an empty stall at 3am, and yet I have watched grown men set down a spoon they wanted very badly to lift.
